passageOrStimulus: A bakery says that extending its opening hours increased daily sales, because revenue rose in the month after the change.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: A large nearby bakery closed during that same month, diverting customers to the bakery in question.
The bakery introduced a new logo.
Customers reported liking the smell of the bread.
The bakery hired one extra cashier.
The town held a food festival that month.

explanation: A nearby competitor closed at the same time, which can explain the sales increase without any connection to the extended hours.

passageOrStimulus: A software team claims that replacing weekly meetings with a shared dashboard will save time without reducing coordination.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Team members will actually check the dashboard regularly and update it accurately.
The dashboard will use bright colors.
Weekly meetings are always boring.
The team works in the same office.
Management prefers dashboards to meetings.

explanation: The argument assumes that the dashboard will actually be consulted and reliably updated.

passageOrStimulus: A clinic introduced a reminder text system and says it reduced missed appointments.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: The same patients had a lower no-show rate after the texts were introduced than before, even after adjusting for season.
Some patients preferred phone calls.
The clinic also painted the waiting room.
Reminder texts cost less than paper notices.
The clinic saw more social-media followers.

explanation: A measured decrease in absences among the same patients directly reinforces the causal link.

passageOrStimulus: A survey found that among people who bought reusable water bottles last year, most also reported carrying a bottle on at least four days per week this year.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: Many recent buyers of reusable bottles are using them regularly this year.
All people who buy reusable bottles use them every day.
Reusable bottles eliminate the need for tap water.
Most people in the survey bought bottles last year.
The survey included only athletes.

explanation: The fact that the majority of buyers report frequent use allows us to infer regular usage among many of them, without generalizing to all.

passageOrStimulus: A city argues that a new protected bike lane reduced traffic congestion because average car travel time on the street fell by 12% after the lane opened.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: A major road nearby was under construction during the same period, causing many drivers to avoid the street for unrelated reasons.
More bicycles were seen on the street after the lane opened.
Some residents like the bike lane.
The lane was painted green.
Parking spaces were removed near the street.

explanation: Road work on another route provides an alternative explanation for the decrease in vehicle traffic.

passageOrStimulus: A museum says that offering a discounted late-night ticket will increase total revenue.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the claim?

choices.text: Whether the discount would attract enough additional visitors to offset the lower ticket price.
Whether the museum's late-night visitors tend to spend more at the gift shop than daytime visitors do.
Whether nearby attractions also offer discounted late-night admission.
Whether the museum has enough staff willing to work late-night shifts.
Whether late-night visitors are more satisfied with their experience than daytime visitors.

explanation: The central issue is whether the additional customer volume offsets the drop in unit price.

passageOrStimulus: A farmer claims that switching to drip irrigation will reduce water use without lowering crop output.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Fields using drip irrigation on similar crops used less water and produced similar yields over the same season.
Drip irrigation systems have become considerably cheaper to install in recent years.
Fields using drip irrigation on similar crops used far less water but produced noticeably smaller yields.
The farmer's region has experienced below-average rainfall for several seasons.
Drip irrigation delivers water directly to the roots of each plant.

explanation: Comparable results with less water and similar yields directly support the claim.

passageOrStimulus: A restaurant says that adding a vegetarian tasting menu will attract new customers without losing existing ones.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Current customers will not view the vegetarian menu as lowering the restaurant's overall quality.
The chef enjoys vegetables.
Vegetarian dishes are usually colorful.
The restaurant is open on Fridays.
Competitors also offer desserts.

explanation: It must be assumed that current customers will not be driven away by the new menu.

passageOrStimulus: A magazine concludes that readers prefer shorter articles because the average time spent on the magazine's website fell after article length was reduced.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.text: The magazine also changed its homepage layout so that articles were more visible and easier to open quickly.
Some articles became shorter.
The website had fewer ads.
Readers complained less about navigation.
The magazine published more photos.

explanation: The layout change provides an alternative explanation for the decrease in time spent on the site.

passageOrStimulus: A company reported that 70% of its remote employees live within commuting distance of an office, and 20% visit an office at least once a week.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: Some remote employees who live near an office do not visit it weekly.
All remote employees live near an office.
Most remote employees visit an office weekly.
No remote employees work from home full time.
The company has only one office.

explanation: If 70% live within commuting distance and only 20% visit each week, some nearby residents do not visit every week.

passageOrStimulus: A neighborhood council claims that planting more trees will lower summer electricity bills.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Homes on tree-lined streets used less air conditioning during hot months than similar homes on treeless streets.
Residents on tree-lined streets reported feeling cooler outdoors in summer.
The proposed trees are a fast-growing species that reaches full height within a few years.
Homes on tree-lined streets had higher heating bills during the winter months.
Mature trees can lower the air temperature in their immediate vicinity.

explanation: Lower air conditioning usage on tree-lined streets supports the link between trees and reduced utility bills.

passageOrStimulus: A company claims that allowing employees to choose their own start times will improve productivity because people work best at different hours.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the claim depends?

choices.text: Employees with flexible start times will still be able to coordinate efficiently with one another.
Most employees prefer coffee to tea.
Adding plants is far less expensive than other measures the office considered.
Managers dislike early meetings.
The company will replace all desks.

explanation: It must be assumed that flexible scheduling will not harm coordination among colleagues.

passageOrStimulus: A school says its new tutoring program improved math scores because students who attended tutoring scored higher on the final exam.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

choices.text: Students who chose tutoring were already the strongest math students before the program began.
Students who attended tutoring also scored higher on the science final.
The school spent more on the tutoring program than it had planned.
Students who skipped tutoring reported studying less for the final exam.
The tutors were drawn from the school's most experienced teachers.

explanation: If enrolled students were already stronger to begin with, the score increase does not prove the program's effectiveness.

passageOrStimulus: A courier company is considering replacing diesel vans with electric vans.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the plan?

choices.text: Whether the electric vans can complete the typical daily route on a single charge.
Whether electric vans produce fewer emissions than the diesel vans they would replace.
Whether the company's drivers are willing to learn to operate electric vans.
Whether electric vans have become more popular among courier companies recently.
Whether the diesel vans could instead be retrofitted to run more efficiently.

explanation: Feasibility depends largely on the actual range of electric vehicles.

passageOrStimulus: A report states that every branch of a chain adopted the same inventory software, but only some branches used it to automate reordering.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: At least one branch used the software without automating reordering.
Most branches that adopted the software automated their reordering.
Every branch that automated reordering did so using the new software.
At least one branch failed to adopt the new inventory software.
Branches that automated reordering held less inventory than those that did not.

explanation: Since all branches adopted the software and only some automated their restocking, at least one branch used it without automation.

passageOrStimulus: An economist argues that a city's minimum-wage increase did not reduce employment because the number of jobs rose afterward.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: The city's population and business activity both grew during the same period.
Some employers said the law was inconvenient.
Workers spent more money at local stores.
The increase was announced in the newspapers.
Other cities debated similar laws.

explanation: Population growth and increased economic activity provide a plausible alternative explanation for the rise in employment.

passageOrStimulus: A gardener says that mulching the soil will reduce watering needs because it slows evaporation.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The mulch will stay in place long enough to affect the soil's moisture.
Mulching will improve the garden's appearance as well as its moisture retention.
The mulch will not raise the cost of maintaining the garden.
Slowing evaporation is the only way to reduce how often the garden must be watered.
The plants in the garden require less water than they currently receive.

explanation: The argument assumes that the mulch remains in place long enough to fulfill its intended purpose.

passageOrStimulus: A hospital claims that a new triage system reduced wait times in the emergency department because the average wait dropped from 54 minutes to 41 minutes.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: During the same period, many low-severity patients were diverted to an urgent-care clinic next door.
Nurses received training on the new system.
The emergency department renovated its entrance.
The hospital's parking lot was repaved.
Patients liked the new signage.

explanation: Diverting minor cases can explain the reduction in wait times without attributing the entire improvement to the new triage process.

passageOrStimulus: A retailer claims that placing products near the checkout line increases impulse purchases.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: Items moved to the checkout line sold more frequently than similar items kept elsewhere in the store.
Total store sales were higher in months when more products were displayed near the checkout.
Customers who waited longest in the checkout line bought the most items overall.
Items placed near the checkout were also featured in the store's weekly advertisements.
Most shoppers say they enjoy browsing the displays near the checkout line.

explanation: More frequent sales after repositioning near the checkout directly support the impulse-buying thesis.

passageOrStimulus: A publisher is deciding whether to print a novel in hardcover only or in both hardcover and paperback.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the decision?

choices.text: Whether the expected paperback demand is high enough to justify the additional printing and distribution costs.
Whether the hardcover edition is expected to sell well on its own.
Whether readers generally prefer paperbacks to hardcovers for novels of this kind.
Whether the paperback would be priced lower than the hardcover.
Whether the publisher's last novel sold more copies in paperback than in hardcover.

explanation: The critical point is whether pocket demand justifies the additional costs.

passageOrStimulus: A city survey showed that among residents who commute by train, most also use a bicycle at least once a week.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: A majority of train commuters use bicycles weekly.
Most residents who use a bicycle weekly also commute by train.
Train commuters use bicycles more often than non-commuters do.
Most train commuters use a bicycle as their primary means of travel.
A majority of the city's residents use a bicycle at least once a week.

explanation: The statement specifically concerns train commuters; it can be inferred that a majority of them also cycle each week.

passageOrStimulus: A manager argues that shortening the quarterly review meeting will not hurt planning because the team already discusses most issues by email.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The issues discussed by email are sufficient to cover the topics that would otherwise require meeting time.
The manager enjoys short meetings.
Employees read every email immediately.
The team meets in a large conference room.
The review meeting always starts on time.

explanation: It must be assumed that email exchanges actually cover the topics that would have required more time in meetings.

passageOrStimulus: A café owner says that introducing self-service kiosks improved customer satisfaction because the average rating increased after the kiosks were installed.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

choices.text: The café simultaneously shortened its menu and removed several unpopular items.
The kiosks had touch screens.
Some customers used the kiosks more than once.
The café added more tables.
Ratings were collected online.

explanation: The improvement in ratings may be due to the simplified menu rather than the kiosks themselves.

passageOrStimulus: A nutritionist claims that people who eat breakfast daily are less likely to snack late at night.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim?

choices.text: In a study, participants who ate breakfast every day reported fewer late-night snacks than similar participants who skipped breakfast.
People who eat breakfast daily tend to be more health-conscious in general.
People who snack late at night often report feeling tired the next morning.
Eating a larger breakfast leaves people feeling fuller throughout the morning.
Among people who already snack at night, those who add breakfast snack just as often.

explanation: A comparative study showing less snacking among people who eat breakfast directly strengthens the conclusion.

passageOrStimulus: A delivery company is deciding whether to shift from same-day to next-day shipping for most orders.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the decision?

choices.text: Whether customers would cancel orders if delivery took one extra day.
Whether next-day shipping would lower the company's delivery costs.
Whether competitors offer same-day shipping on most orders.
Whether the company can deliver next-day orders on schedule.
Whether most current orders are already delivered the next day in practice.

explanation: The essential issue is how customers will react to an additional one-day delay.

passageOrStimulus: A student says that studying in the library is better than studying at home because the library is quieter.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The student can actually focus better in quiet places.
The library is open every day.
Books are available at the library.
The student owns a laptop.
Home internet is slow.

explanation: The argument must assume that quiet surroundings actually improve this student's concentration.

passageOrStimulus: A report says that all of a firm's interns received training, but only half received mentors.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: At least one intern received training without a mentor.
No intern received both training and a mentor.
All interns had mentors.
Some interns were not trained.
The firm hired only one intern.

explanation: Since all received training and only some have a mentor, at least one trainee received training without a mentor.

passageOrStimulus: A manufacturer claims that changing the packaging of its cereal increased sales because sales rose after the redesign.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The cereal was also placed on a lower shelf where shoppers were more likely to notice it.
The new package used brighter colors.
Some customers said they liked the old logo.
The redesign took six months.
The cereal was sold in several stores.

explanation: The shelf repositioning provides a competing explanation for the increase in sales.

passageOrStimulus: A school district argues that later start times will improve attendance because more students arrive late on early-start days.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument?

choices.text: On days when classes started later, the district recorded fewer tardy arrivals than on comparable early-start days.
On days when classes started later, students reported getting more sleep the night before.
On days when classes started later, fewer students were absent for the entire day.
Neighboring districts that adopted later start times saw their test scores improve.
Most parents in the district favor moving to a later start time.

explanation: A direct comparison showing fewer delays with a later start time supports the conclusion.

passageOrStimulus: A nonprofit is considering a fundraising dinner.
stem: Which of the following would be most useful to evaluate the plan?

choices.text: Whether the expected ticket sales would cover the cost of the venue and catering.
Whether the dinner would attract more attendees than last year's event.
Whether donors generally respond better to dinners than to mailed appeals.
Whether the nonprofit can secure a discounted rate on the venue.
Whether the dinner would raise the nonprofit's profile in the community.

explanation: The decisive criterion is that the projected revenue covers the cost of the event.

passageOrStimulus: An app developer claims that adding a dark mode will reduce eye strain because users spend many hours on the app at night.
stem: Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

choices.text: The app is bright enough that reducing screen brightness would matter to users.
Most users live in apartments.
The app already has a search feature.
Users prefer free apps.
The developer can update the app quickly.

explanation: The argument assumes that the current screen is bright enough for dark mode to have a real effect on eye strain.

passageOrStimulus: A company's annual report says that revenue rose in every quarter, while expenses rose only in the first two quarters.
stem: Which of the following can be inferred?

choices.text: There was at least one quarter in which profit improved relative to the previous quarter.
Expenses never rose.
Revenue always exceeded expenses by the same amount.
The company had no debt.
Revenue grew more slowly than expenses in all quarters.

explanation: If revenue increased every quarter and expenses only rose at the start, there is at least one quarter in which profitability improved.

passageOrStimulus: An insurer says that its new phone-based screening system reduced fraudulent claims because the share of suspicious claims declined after it was introduced.
stem: Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

choices.text: The system was introduced at the same time a new law imposed heavier penalties for fraud.
The total number of claims the insurer received rose after the system was introduced.
The screening system was more expensive to operate than the insurer had expected.
Most policyholders said the phone-based screening was easy to complete.
A rival insurer adopted a similar screening system the following year.

explanation: A new law with stricter penalties provides another plausible reason for the decline in suspicious requests.
